result:
The first thing I notice when I wake up is the color of the air. Not the temperature, not the texture of the sheets against my calves, not the digital red 7:03 on the clock, but the color. Some mornings it arrives the shade of a bruised peach, others a thin, almost metallic silver that tastes like a coin held too long on the tongue. Today it is the dull yellow of old piano keys, a color that creaks. I lie still and let it settle on my corneas while the rest of the house decides what kind of existence it will entertain. The refrigerator coughs. The neighbor’s dog practices a small, obsessive arpeggio of barks. Somewhere a phone vibrates against a wooden surface, an insect trapped between layers of varnish and time.

I used to think that adulthood would feel like a finished novel, hardbound and smelling of fresh glue, all pages accounted for. Instead it feels like a document I keep reopening, cursor blinking mid-sentence, unsure whether I’m revising or merely treading water in the margins. At thirty-six I have a job title that sounds fictional—Senior Narrative Designer—which means I scatter breadcrumbs of story across corporate software so that strangers feel something approximating wonder when they click “submit claim.” The irony is not lost on me: I went to graduate school to write the Great American Novel and emerged with a salary for hiding parables inside dropdown menus. My mother keeps asking when I’ll publish “a real book.” I tell her I’m still accumulating material, but the truth is that every year the material accumulates me.

There is a small crack in the bedroom ceiling that has been migrating slowly southward like a glacier in miniature. I watch it the way sailors once watched stars, trying to divine whether the journey is one of departure or return. My partner, Eli, sleeps perpendicular to me, having spun 90 degrees sometime after two a.m., a maneuver I registered only subconsciously when I dreamed of a freighter turning sideways in a narrow canal. Eli’s foot sticks out from the duvet, five toes in a perfect row, each nail painted a different color—last night’s bored binge of “intersectional pedicure,” they called it. The polish is already chipped, evidence of how quickly beauty consents to entropy. I resist the urge to place my thumb on the arch of that foot, to press gently as if it were a doorbell that might ring somewhere inside their dream.

Instead I get up, negotiate the dark corridor, and greet the cat—officially named Sorghum, unofficially addressed as Your Highness of Hairballs. She presents her anus to me with the pride of a magician revealing a chosen card. I bow, accepting the invitation to witness the universal sphincter, that microscopic black hole around which all feline cosmology revolves. In the kitchen I start the kettle and open my laptop to the overnight accumulation of panic: newsletters about the collapse of insects, photos of my ex’s newborn dressed in a onesie that says “Already Smarter Than Congress,” invitations to webinars on how to monetize my personal brand. I delete twenty-three emails, keep one headed “Your data is safe with us,” and feel the small thrill of pressing the trash icon, as if each swipe erases not only spam but a sliver of the self who once believed she needed to answer everything.

While the water heats I practice a ritual stolen from a writer I used to idolize: I write one sentence on an index card, no matter how awkward, and tape it to the cabinet door. Yesterday’s still clings there: “The moon is a liar, but we love her anyway.” Today I write: “Beware of any revolution that does not leave room for afternoon naps.” The pen skips on the word “revolution,” leaving a tiny hole in the paper, a window into the cardboard beyond. I imagine minuscule guerillas peering through it, planning their miniature coup, their manifestos folded into paper airplanes that will glide down onto the countertop and demand crusts of bread.

By 7:45 I am on the train, squeezed between a man reading Braille with one hand and scrolling Twitter with the other, and a woman wearing headphones shaped like cat ears, the felt tips twitching in sync with whatever beat she's swallowing. I pretend I am inside a mobile terrarium, a specimen of homo commutus, fed on podcasts and anxiety. The window reflects my face back at me overlaid on the passing city, so that my eyes become the upper floors of an office building, my mouth the dark vacancy of a parking garage. Somewhere around 42nd the train stalls and the conductor apologizes for “a situation ahead.” We never learn what the situation is; perhaps it is merely the future, stubbornly blocking the present. A collective sigh rises, carbon dioxide and unwritten resignation letters. I open my notebook and write: “We are all rehearsal for the moment we finally get off.”

At the office I ride the elevator with Deborah from Compliance, who smells of cinnamon gum and existential dread. She informs me that the company will now be measuring “employee emotional velocity” via an app that pings us three times a day to ask how energized we feel on a scale of one to five. “It’s gamified!” she chirps, as if gamified is the word for minor floggings redesigned by Disneyland. I congratulate her on the impending quantification of despair and exit before she can recruit me to the pilot program. My cubicle—sorry, “flexible productivity pod”—sits beside a window that does not open overlooking another window that does not open. Between us, a shaft of airless light where pigeons execute mating dances on the sill. I once saw one try to copulate with a discarded chicken wing, an avian ouroboros, the city devouring itself in breaded form.

The morning dissolves into meetings about “story-driven user journeys.” My team argues over whether the animated gecko who guides customers through the insurance portal should have a tragic backstory. Marketing wants childhood trauma in the Galapagos; Legal worries about implied species-wide PTSD. I suggest the gecko merely questions the concept of indemnity itself, staring into the mirror each morning whispering, “What is damage, really?” The call ends with no resolution, only shared screen fatigue. I type up my notes: “Existential gecko = brand risk,” and reward myself with a walk to the water fountain, where I count how many seconds the auto-sensor requires to acknowledge my cup (four), how many to stop once I remove it (seven). The machine is more reluctant to let go than most lovers I’ve known.

Back at my desk I discover an email from my father, subject line: “Thought you’d enjoy this.” Inside is a photo of a box of my old notebooks rescued from the attic, their covers sun-bleached to the hues of discontinued candy. Beneath the image he has typed: “Your mother wants to recycle these. Flagging for your review.” I study the thumbnail: spiral spines splayed like roadkill, pages swollen with humidity and puberty. I remember writing inside one of them, age thirteen, “I want to live so hard that death gets jealous.” At that moment I believed intensity was a form of immortality; I had not yet met the way time softens even the sharpest exclamation into something resembling background music. I forward the email to myself with the subject “Crisis of Archive,” then open a new tab to watch a thirty-second video of a raccoon stealing a donut from a police station, because balance.

At lunch I sit in the communal area with Kai, who designs virtual plants—pixels arranged to resemble foliage for people whose apartments cannot sustain the real thing. They tell me sales are booming ever since the wildfires made air itself feel speculative. “We’re working on a fern that doubles as a mood ring,” they say, biting into a burrito the size of a newborn. I confess that I have started talking to the pothos on my windowsill, asking it for stock tips. The plant has not responded, but it hasn’t died either, which in this economy counts as bullish. We trade stories of other people’s delusions: the VP who insists her aura is being drained by Slack notifications, the intern who tried to expense a psychic. Somewhere on another floor a fire alarm practices its aria; no one moves. We are all evacuated in place, waiting for an emergency we can monetize.

The afternoon presents its usual conspiracy of micro-tasks. I tweak the tooltip text for a dropdown menu so that hovering over “deductible” now displays: “Like regret, but with numbers.” My manager praises the whimsical touch; Legal requests a citation. I wonder what it would feel like to write something that did not require approval from a committee whose youngest member was born the year Titanic won Oscars. I open a blank document and type: “Once upon a time there was a hole in the shape of a person.” I stare at the sentence until the letters rearrange themselves into the company logo. Copyright law is stronger than magic.

At 5:03 p.m. I join the exodus, a river of lanyards and posture damage. On the street I pass a man wearing a sandwich board that reads “The End Is High-Resolution” and handing out QR codes. I scan one out of habit; it leads to a 404 page titled “Apocalypse Not Found.” I think about how even doom has become user-tested, optimized for maximum click-through. In the subway a mariachi band boards, three men and a woman in sequined jackets singing about heartbreak in a language I half understand. The accordion folds and unfolds like a paper lung. A toddler drops a plastic dinosaur, which lands on its side; the T-rex appears to be listening, its tiny arms forever caught mid-applause. I want to believe this is an omen, but omen implies narrative, and the city mostly offers reruns.

Back home Eli has cooked something involving chickpeas and the illusion of organization. They recount their day: a Zoom séance for a museum that wants to “crowdsource curation from the astral plane.” Apparently a nineteenth-century spirit recommended more abstract expressionism, but the board worried dead painters might compete with living donors. We eat on the couch, bowls balanced on our knees, watching a reality show where contestants redesign their childhood traumas into luxury treehouses. The winner gets therapy and a sponsorship deal. During a commercial I ask Eli if they think we’re getting better at being human or just more efficient at simulating improvement. They shrug, lick tahini from their thumb, and say, “Maybe those are the same thing in beta.”

After dinner we attempt the crossword. Today’s theme is “Obsolete Futures.” A clue reads: “1980s promise that never arrived (two words).” We try “jet pack,” “cold fusion,” “paperless office,” nothing fits. Finally Eli writes “honest politics,” which is too long but earns a kiss anyway. The cat walks across the grid, leaving paw prints that look like Morse code for “feed me.” We obey, because democracy ended when we domesticated predators smaller than our cynicism.

Later, in bed, Eli falls asleep reading a novel that smells like our library’s dehumidifier. I scroll on my phone until the screen becomes a rectangular sun I can’t stop orbiting. An article informs me that the moon is drifting away from Earth at the rate of 3.78 centimeters per year, the width of an iPhone 14. I picture future lovers trying to point at a sky increasingly empty, telling their kids, “There used to be a nightlight.” I set the phone down and press my forehead to the window glass, cold as the other side of a fever. Across the alley a neighbor irons shirts at midnight, steam rising like ghost after ghost wearing business attire. I imagine each shirt going to job interviews for bodies that no longer exist.

Some nights I feel my thoughts arranging themselves into a poem, but the lines arrive already footnoted, hyperlinked, fact-checked into inertia. I remember the index card on the cabinet: “Beware of any revolution that does not leave room for afternoon naps.” I add a corollary in my head: “And for lying awake counting the moon’s small disappearances.” Tomorrow I will wake to the color of old piano keys again, ride the train past buildings that do not know my name, embed koans in insurance portals. I will try to write one true sentence before the kettle boils, delete emails that promise to protect the planet by switching to paperless billing, watch the ceiling crack migrate like a bored astronomer drawing new constellations. I will fail, in small ways, to become the person my notebooks prophesied, and succeed, in smaller ways, at remaining curious about the texture of the failure.

Outside, the city hums like a hard drive that has forgotten what file it was meant to save. Somewhere a siren starts and stops, a sentence interrupted by its own urgency. I think about the hole-shaped person from the blank document, wonder if she is waiting for me to step inside, or if she is already wearing my clothes, commuting home under a moon that inches away each year, gentle as a parent who knows the child must someday leave. I close my eyes and measure the distance: one iPhone, two iPhones, three. Enough for a constellation of glass and aluminum, a belt of obsolete futures orbiting the planet of my chest. The alarm will go off at seven. The air will decide what color to be. I will practice breathing in that shade, whatever it is, until it feels less like borrowed light and more like a sentence I haven’t yet finished revising.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 2500 words.
